coredata.dc:title: A novel sensitive aptamer-based nanosensor using rGQDs and MWCNTs for rapid detection of diazinon pesticide
coredata.dc:creator: Talari, Fateme Fooladi
Bozorg, Ali
Faridbod, Farnoush
Vossoughi, Manouchehr
coredata.dc:description: An optical apta-nanosensor was designed and developed based on reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs) and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and applied for the selective detection of diazinon as one of the most widely used organophosphorus pesticides. Considering the GQDs and rGQDs high fluorescence emission and optical stability, efficient optical transducers could be designed and precise detection methods could be developed based on such transducers. Herein, by using rGQDs, diazinon specific aptamer, and MWCNTs, a simple economical fluorescence method has been introduced to detect and measure diazinon with the detection limit of 0.4 nM (0.1 µg/L) in the range of 4–31 nM, meeting the specifications and standards set by the European Union and World Health Organization. To assess apta-nanosensor performance, the real application of the novel apta-nanosensor was then validated by detecting and quantifying diazinon in real samples, including tap water, urine, river water, and agricultural runoff water. The obtained results revealed that, due to the lack of binding affinity for the diazinon specific aptamer, the emission recoveries were kept intact in the presence of other pesticides and thereby, the designed apta-nanosensor was able to promptly detect diazinon pesticide with high accuracy and selectivity in real samples. Accordingly, the accurate apta-nanosensor developed in this study provides a sensitive, inexpensive, fast, and portable device for selective detection of diazinon over other pesticides in contaminated regions. The introduced innovative method could also be applied for other pesticides, providing a practical on-site detection approach for real samples of multi pesticides.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organophosphorus pesticides
Biosensor
Apta-nanosensor
rGQDs
MWCNTs
Optical transducer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: GQDs and rGQDs structures and synthesis process.
A) Fluorescence emission of GQDs and rGQDs, B) UV-Vis spectra of GQDs and rGQDs, C) Emission of different concentrations of rGQDs under UV light, and D) TEM image of rGQDs.
A) FT-IR analysis of GQDs and rGQDs, B) Raman spectra of GQDs and rGQDs, C) C1s XPS data of GQDs and rGQDs, D) Full-range XPS spectra of GQDs and rGQDs.
Effect of different parameters on the emission of apta-nanosensor: A) rGQDs concentration, B) Aptamer, C) pH, D) MWCNTs, and E) Excitation wavelength.
A) Effect of aptamer and MWCNTs on rGQDs, B) The apta-nanosensor responses against the diazinon concentration in the range of 1–30 nM, and C) Calibration curve.
Descriptive schematic of rGQDs/labeled aptamer preparation, aptamer-rGQDs/MWCNTs assembly, and diazinon determination at enhanced fluorescence intensity.
Fluorescence intensities achieved in the presence of 10 nM of different commonly used pesticides (fenthion, dichlorvos, bentazon, pirimicarb, malathion, and deltamethrin).
Diazinon concentrations determined by the developed apta-nanosensor in real samples compared to the results achieved by the HPLC (n = 4).
The physical and chemical parameters of real samples.
Comparison of the developed apta-nanosensor performance with some other methods reported for diazinon detection.

head.ce:title: A novel sensitive aptamer-based nanosensor using rGQDs and MWCNTs for rapid detection of diazinon pesticide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biotechnology Department, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Biotechnology and Environment (IBE), Sharif University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Biotechnology and Environment (IBE), Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An optical apta-nanosensor was designed and developed based on reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs) and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and applied for the selective detection of diazinon as one of the most widely used organophosphorus pesticides. Considering the GQDs and rGQDs high fluorescence emission and optical stability, efficient optical transducers could be designed and precise detection methods could be developed based on such transducers. Herein, by using rGQDs, diazinon specific aptamer, and MWCNTs, a simple economical fluorescence method has been introduced to detect and measure diazinon with the detection limit of 0.4 nM (0.1 µg/L) in the range of 4–31 nM, meeting the specifications and standards set by the European Union and World Health Organization. To assess apta-nanosensor performance, the real application of the novel apta-nanosensor was then validated by detecting and quantifying diazinon in real samples, including tap water, urine, river water, and agricultural runoff water. The obtained results revealed that, due to the lack of binding affinity for the diazinon specific aptamer, the emission recoveries were kept intact in the presence of other pesticides and thereby, the designed apta-nanosensor was able to promptly detect diazinon pesticide with high accuracy and selectivity in real samples. Accordingly, the accurate apta-nanosensor developed in this study provides a sensitive, inexpensive, fast, and portable device for selective detection of diazinon over other pesticides in contaminated regions. The introduced innovative method could also be applied for other pesticides, providing a practical on-site detection approach for real samples of multi pesticides.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus (OP) pesticides (e.g. insecticides, herbicides, and fungicides) are neurotoxic compounds [1–3] that, according to the Environmental Protection Agency classification [4,5], belong to the toxicity class I (highly toxic) or toxicity class II (moderately toxic). Such compounds are widely used in agricultural industries to control and eliminate pests that can reduce productivity and damage crops and livestock worldwide [1,4]. The toxicity of OP pesticides is due to the irreversible inhibition of the acetylcholinesterase enzyme that is essential for the function of the central nervous system of mammals and insects. By doing so, the neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ACh) would be accumulated and interfere with muscular responses leading to respiratory muscle failure, myocardial impairment, or even death [1,4,6].

Diazinon ( Fig. 1) is one of the OP pesticides that has been widely used against insects in agricultural and household practices [7,8]. Like other OP pesticides, diazinon inhibits acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity, and thereby, due to the AChE accumulation in the synaptic cleft at muscarinic, nicotinic, and central sites, leading to multiple toxic effects such as convulsions, headache, delirium, dizziness, and depression. Other toxic effects of diazinon on hepatocytes, spleen, blood cells, thymus, lymph nodes, and heart have also been reported in humans and animals [8,9]. Due to such detrimental effects, the European Union and World Health Organization have specified maximum residue limits (MRLs) of 0.1 µg/L, 0.04 µg/kg, and 0.04 µg/g diazinon in soil, water, and vegetables, respectively [10,11].

High amounts of diazinon in surface waters have been reported in different studies [12,13]. Furthermore, many farmers would be exposed to high levels of pesticides, including diazinon which could not only lead to an increased presence of diazinon in their bodies but also in the agricultural runoff water that eventually ends in rivers. Accordingly, as the amounts detected have been far higher than those currently recommended by the European Union and World Health Organization [8], diazinon detection would be of utmost importance in such regions. Different analytical methods, such as FT-Raman spectroscopy [14], spectrophotometry [15], chromatography (HPLC, HPTLC, LC/MS, GC, GC/MS) [16–25], immunoassays [26–28], biosensors [29–31], and electrochemical methods [32–37] have been used to detect and quantify diazinon. However, although these conventional methods have been successfully used in the detection of OP pesticides, they suffer from several disadvantages, including offsite sample analysis, lengthy analysis times, expensive instrumentation, sample pre-treatment, and complicated procedures need to be carried out by the experts. This implies that field-deployable analytical methods providing simple, reliable, economic, sensitive, selective, and fast detection and measurement of diazinon at low concentrations would be practically important to manage the spread of diazinon in contaminated regions [4].

Biosensors are analytical devices combining a physical transducer with a biochemical recognition component that use a biological specificity to detect a target compound [7]. Biosensors have been increasingly used to detect the compounds with specific characteristics due to their simplicity, advanced selectivity, and high sensitivity. Based on such unique features, biosensors have demonstrated great applicability in the detection of OP pesticides and have been considered as promising alternatives to conventional detection methods. Aptamers (i.e. artificial antibody) are single-strand DNA or RNA sequences or peptides selected from the systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) that can be used in biosensors to specifically detect different targets such as drugs, proteins, peptides, toxicants, vitamins, and cells [38–41]. When replaced for antibodies, aptamers can provide the biosensors with advantages like lower cost, more feasible manufacturing, more convenient implementation, thermal resistance, chemical stability, and reduced response time [10,42,43]. On the other hand, to achieve a biosensor of high performance, the sensitive and specific biochemical recognition compartment needs to be incorporated by a robust physical transducer. Recently, nanotechnology advances have provided great opportunities to synthesize materials with desired characteristics to be used in biosensors [44].

The sensors that possess graphene quantum dots (GQDs) probe as transducer have revealed excellent application prospect in in vivo and in vitro detection of toxic compounds [45,46] and have been considered as promising solutions for continuous environmental monitoring [47]. Based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), GQDs have been employed extensively as fluorescent labels in sensors [48,49]. Accordingly, by conjugating the GQDs to different biomolecules like aptamers, FRET can be used to construct advanced apta-nanosensors of high performance [48,50].

Herein, an aptamer was designed to specifically detect and quantify diazinon pesticide and an amine labeled aptamer was employed to improve its reproducibility. 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was used to immobilize the diazinon aptamer on rGQDs [51]. By using rGQDs, a novel apta-nanosensor was then designed and constructed for diazinon pesticide detection in real samples and its accuracy, selectivity, and specificity assessed in detail.

All chemicals used in this study, including citric acid, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), tetrahydrofuran, methanol, ethanol, diazinon, parathion, malathion, pirimicarb (purity 98%), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), NaOH, EDC-HCl, NHS, and Tris-HCl were obtained from Merck Chemical Co. Amine labeled DF20 aptamer (5′-NH2-(CH2)6ATCCGTCACACCTGCTCTAATATAGAGGTATTGCTCTTGGACAAGGTACAGGGATGGTGTTGGCTCCCGTAT) oligonucleotides (100 µM), purified by HPLC, was purchased from Pishgam Company (Tehran, Iran). The stock solution of the aptamer (1 µM) was prepared by PBS (pH=7) and kept at − 20 °C.

A Perkin-Elmer LS50 luminescence spectrometer was used in fluorescence studies. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Philips CM 10 HT, 300 kv, Japan) was used to determine the size of the rGQDs and apta-nanosensor. UV–Vis spectra and absorbance measurements were obtained using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu 1650 PC, Japan). FT-IR data were also collected by Perkin Elmer Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. To be well mixed, the samples were vortexed by Vortex Mixer (Gemmy VM-300) for 5 min prior to be analyzed. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured (Vestech-Germany) to determine the chemical compositions and their relative amounts. Raman spectra were also performed at 532 nm (DeltaNu Advantage) to confirm the formation of GQDs and rGQDs.

GQDs were synthesized by citric acid pyrolysis [52,53]. To do so, while being heated on a magnet stirrer at 250 °C, 3 g citric acid was dissolved in a 25 ml beaker for about 20 min until its color turned to orange. The rGQDs were then obtained by adding 1.6 g GQDs and excessive amount of NaBH4 into 20 ml tetrahydrofuran and stirring the solution at 70 °C for 8 h ( Fig. 2) [44]. The synthesized rGQDs were finally isolated from the mixture to remove additional sodium borohydride using vacuum-distillation and washed twice with ethanol.

The optimum amount of 0.6 mg rGQDs powder was added to 3 ml phosphate buffer. To activate carboxylic groups, EDC and NHS (1:1, 50 mg: 50 mg) were added to the solution, mixed for 45 min under magnetic stirring, and then, the labeled standard aptamer solution (1 µM DF20) was included and stirred for 24 h. Subsequently, 10 µl MWCNTs was added at the predetermined optimum concentration to enhance detection precision. The obtained mixture was finally centrifuged and washed with PBS buffer for several times.

The stock solution of rGQDs was diluted by phosphate buffer and subsequently, 100 µl aptamer (1 µM) was included. After 1 h, MWCNTs were added and the obtained solution was sonicated to a homogeneous dispersion. Different amounts of standard diazinon solution (0.3 µM) were then sequentially injected into the cell. A 4.5 ml quartz cell with 10 mm width was used to assess the obtained aptamer-rGQDs probe emission. According to the obtained results (Sections 3.1), the probe emission was at 460 nm, while the excitation wavelength was adjusted at 378 nm.

Samples of tap water (Tehran, Iran), water from the Tajan river (Mazandaran, Iran), agricultural runoff water (Mazandaran, Iran), and human urine were collected and assessed to determine the applicability of the developed apta-nanosensor for diazinon detection in real samples. As all the samples were clear and transparent, no further preparation was required. Samples were divided and spiked with 5 different diazinon concentrations and used to verify the developed apta-nanosensor performance ( Table 1).

Diazinon (99% Merck Chemical Co) was diluted to the final concentrations of 30, 3, and 0.3 µM. The prepared rGQDs-Aptamer-MWCNTs solution was placed in the quartz cell and then the standard diazinon solutions were included. After 5 min incubation at room temperature, the cells were placed in a spectrometer to record the responses and ultimately, diazinon was titred in the cells. By being excited at 378 nm, the fluorescence measurement of apta-nanosensor was carried out at 460 nm.

In this study, to be used as a luminescent probe, GQDs was reduced by sodium borohydride to achieve a robust nano-biosensor. As a simple and effective process, chemical reduction has been used extensively in different studies to enhance fluorescence emission and improve the optical properties of carbon nano-materials. The characterization of rGQDs has been shown in Fig. 3. As illustrated in Fig. 3A, the reduction of GQDs could be confirmed by the more symmetrical emission of rGQDs at a shorter wavelength (i.e. blue shift) compared to that of the GQDs.

Pyrolysis of citric acid would actually lead to the formation of graphene oxide quantum dots. Accordingly, a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carbonyl, epoxy, and carboxyl groups would present within the free nonbonding electrons, resulting in a broad-spectrum. However, after being reduced to rGQDs, the number of disturbance-induced states within n-π* transition would decrease, thereby enhancing the luminescence emission. Such differences between rGQDs and GQDs luminescence properties could be observed in the obtained UV-Vis spectra (Fig. 3B). Fig. 3C also demonstrates the emission of rGQDs of different concentrations under UV light. In addition, similar morphology and size distribution of the rGQDs were confirmed through the TEM (Fig. 3D) images.

The FT-IR analysis illustrated in Fig. 4A also confirmed the formation of rGQDs. As demonstrated, the peaks at 3283 and 2635 cm−1 were respectively attributed to the –OH groups and C-H stretching vibration which could be observed in both spectra. In addition, the stretching vibration of carbonyl group at 1668 cm−1 was lower in rGQDs, and the peak of epoxy groups (-C-O-C-) was disappeared after reduction. Also, as shown in Raman spectra (Fig. 4B), although G and D bands were observed in both GQDs and rGQDs at respectively 1593 cm−1 and 1365 cm−1, the D to G ratio was increased in rGQDs. As shown in XPS data (Fig. 4C), in contrast to C-O, the percentage of C˭O and C˭C decreased in rGQDs compared to that of the GQDs. As depicted in Figs. 4D, 2 dominant peaks of O1s peak at ca. 531.7 eV and C1s peak at ca. 285.2 eV were detected in GQDs and rGQDs XPS spectra. The lower intensity of O1s peak after reduction by NaBH4 in rGQDs indicated that some oxygenous groups were removed through the reduction process. All the characterizations indicated the correct synthesis of GQDs and rGQDs and confirmed the successful reduction of GQDs to rGQDs by using NaBH4.

Based on the performances obtained for the apta-nanosensor, different parameters including the concentrations of rGQDs, aptamer, MWCNTs, pH, and incubation time were assessed to achieve the best possible efficiency for diazinon detection. To assess the optimum time of incubation, rGQDs and aptamer were incubated for different time intervals, ranging from 10 min to 3 h. As confirmed experimentally, 1 h incubation was found to be sufficient to get proper responses. In the presence of diazinon, the probe was also able to provide appropriate responses in 10 min. According to the obtained experimental results, fluorescent emission was directly related to rGQDs and lowered by reducing the rGQDs concentration ( Fig. 5A). To verify the best aptamer concentration, different volumes of the aptamer stock solution (1 µM) ranging from 50 µl to 250 µl were added to the rGQDs solution. Based on the obtained results, although higher aptamer concentrations could lead to enhanced quenching (Fig. 5B), 100 µl was determined to be enough to get sufficient quenching.

In addition, in terms of pH, it was concluded that the emission would be stimulated in alkaline pH by increasing pH from 3 to 9 (Fig. 5C). By using a range of MWCNTs concentrations, it was also concluded that the presence of MWCNTs in rGQDs-aptamer solution could effectively quench the fluorescence emission. Therefore, 10 µl (0.75 g/l) was used throughout the experiments to ensure sufficient quenching (Fig. 5D). However, as the optimum pH of the target diazinon was from 4 to 8, working at alkaline pH was not applicable. Finally, to determine the most effective excitation wavelength, samples were excited at different wavelengths and the best excitation wavelength was found to be 378 nm (Fig. 5E).

Fluorescence analysis is one of the most favorable techniques in aptamer-based biosensor designing. Compared to other detection methods, fluorescence detection has many desired features such as simplicity, rapid analysis, high efficiency, and sensitivity. In this study, by adding different concentrations of diazinon to the Aptamer-rGQDs-MWCNTs ( Fig. 6A), the fluorescent emission was enhanced due to the attraction of aptamer to the target (Fig. 6B). A descriptive schematic of such fluorescence mechanism has been demonstrated in Fig. 7. A linear relationship (R2 = 0.9913) between the concentration of diazinon and the fluorescence emission could be detected in the calibration curve (Fig. 6C). The linear range was from 4 nM to 31 nM diazinon, the Limit of Detection (LOD) was found to be as low as 0.4 nM, and the Quantification Limit was almost 4 nM. In addition, as confirmed experimentally, the results could be consistently reproduced with 97% accuracy by using the obtained apta-nanosensor. By following the same procedures, diazinon certified reference material (TraceCERT®, 333-41-5) was also used to confirm the accuracy of the proposed apta-nanosensor. The predetermined concentration of the diazinon reference material was diluted in methanol (certified value = 25 nM), and the concentration was evaluated using the apta-nanosensor. Based on the obtained results, as confirmed by the t-test, there was no significant difference between the measured (25.6 ± 0.2 nM) and certified values.

To verify the selectivity of the obtained apta-nanosensor, fluorescence response of the apta-nanosensor was assessed in the presence of 10 nM of the other pesticides including fenthion, dichlorvos, bentazon, pirimicarb, malathion, and deltamethrin. As the selectivity of the apta-nanosensor was based on the selective interaction between the diazinon specific aptamer sequence (DF 20) and diazinon, the apta-nanosensor did not respond to any of the other pesticides ( Fig. 8). Therefore, it could be concluded that the other pesticides did not interfere with diazinon detection. Different ions interfering mentioned in Table 2 was studied using real samples that naturally contain ions and other substances. In the presence of other pesticides and different ions, diazinon could be detected with an acceptable 3% RSD.

Real samples of tap water, urine, river water, and agricultural runoff water that inherently contain different ions and substances were used to assess the matrix effect on the apta-nanosensor performance. Five different concentrations of diazinon, ranging from 0 to 40 nM were used for each sample to verify the possible interferences that medium characteristics could have on diazinon detection in real samples. The physical and chemical properties of the samples have been listed in Table 2. Also, as summarized in Table 1, the relative standard deviation (RSD) varied from 1% to 3% and the fluorescence recoveries were all above 97%, implying the capability of the novel apta-nanosensor in diazinon detection in real samples with all possible interferences. Furthermore, the amounts of diazinon in real samples were quantified by the HPLC method for more validation. As shown, there was a good agreement between the amounts determined by the apta-nanosensor and ones reported by the HPLC (Table 1).

Finally, to verify the applicability of the proposed method in diazinon detection, a comparison was made between the performance of the developed apta-nanosensor and some other methods reported in literature ( Table 3). As shown, the LOD achieved by the introduced apta-nanosensor was lower than the other methods listed in Table 3, confirming the precision of the novel optical apta-nanosensor in diazinon detection. Furthermore, based on the obtained experimental results, the proposed detection method provides reliable results in real samples of different matrices and thus, could be possibly considered to detect diazinon in the samples including wide ranges of substances.

Interaction between rGQDs, MWCNTs, and a specific labeled aptamer (DF20) was used for the first time to develop a sensitive apta-nanosensor for the detection of diazinon pesticide in water. Based on the experimental results, diazinon concentrations as low as 0.4 nM could be detected, indicating a very low LOD for this novel apta-nanosensor. In the absence of diazinon, the conjugation of aptamer-rGQDs and MWCNTs resulted in reduced emission. However, the MWCNTs and aptamer-rGQDs complex could then be separated by adding diazinon, leading to the increased emission. Ultimately, by adding diazinon, MWCNTs were separated and the emission was enhanced. Strong fluorescence properties, high biocompatibility, and resistance to photobleaching were some of the important practical features of the probe with an aptamer. Due to the efficient self-assembly between rGQDs and carbon nanotubes, fluorescence resonance energy transfer could also be easily achieved. Based on the obtained results, the developed apta-nanosensor was able to detect diazinon with high sensitivity and good reproducibility, implying that, the portable non-toxic aptamer-based biosensors could be considered to promptly detect organophosphate pesticides on-site with high stability, specificity, and selectivity. Considering the high compatibility of the biosensor components and not having the limitations of enzymes and immunosensors, such as lack of specificity and instability, the introduced detection method could have real applications in a variety of contexts by providing a powerful approach for multi-pesticide detection.

Fateme Fooladi Talari: Investigation, Validation, Writing - original draft. Ali Bozorg: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Farnoush Faridbod: Conceptualization, Data curation, Resources. Manouchehr Vossoughi: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their appreciation to the University of Tehran Research Council, especially the Institute of Electrochemistry, for financial and technical support of this work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of organophosphorus pesticides using potentiometric enzymatic membrane biosensor based on methylcellulose immobilization
Anal. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhang, A. Luo, P. Liu, S. Wei, G. Wang, S. Wei, Detection of organophosphorus pesticides using potentiometric enzymatic membrane biosensor based on methylcellulose immobilization, Anal. Sci. 25 (2009) 511–515. https://doi.org/10.2116/analsci.25.511.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent peptide probes for organophosphorus pesticides detection
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wang, J. Zhang, J. Wang, G. Fang, J. Liu, S. Wang, Fluorescent peptide probes for organophosphorus pesticides detection, Journal of Hazardous Materials 389 (2020) 122074. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2020.122074.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescence-based biosensor for the detection of organophosphate pesticides and chemical warfare agents
Sens. Actuators, B Chem.

Talanta

Intell. Biosens.

Anal. Biochem.

Anal. Bioanal. Chem.

Chem. 408 (2016) 6769–6779. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00216‐016‐9802‐7.

Food Chem

Biosens. Bioelectron.
